elements.sent-2411-001: Deploying robots in open-world environments involves complex tasks characterized by long sequences and rich interactions, necessitating efficient transfer of robotic skills across diverse and complex scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-002: To address this challenge, we propose a skill library framework based on knowledge graphs, which endows robots with high-level skill awareness and spatial semantic understanding.
elements.sent-2411-003: The framework hierarchically organizes operational knowledge by constructing a ``task graph" and a ``scene graph" to represent task and scene semantic information, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce a ``state graph" to facilitate interaction between high-level task planning and low-level scene information.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we propose a hierarchical transfer framework for operational skills.
elements.sent-2411-006: At the task level, the framework integrates contextual learning and chain-of-thought prompting within a four-stage prompt paradigm, leveraging large language models' (LLMs) reasoning and generalization capabilities to achieve task-level subtask sequence transfer.
elements.sent-2411-007: At the motion level, an adaptive trajectory transfer method is developed using the A* algorithm and the skill library, enabling motion-level adaptive trajectory transfer.
elements.sent-2411-008: At the physical level, we introduce an adaptive contour extraction and posture perception method based on tactile perception.
elements.sent-2411-009: This method dynamically obtains high-precision contour and posture information from visual-tactile texture data and adjusts transferred skills, such as contact positions and postures, to ensure effectiveness in new environments.
elements.sent-2411-010: Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.. Project website: \url{https://github.com/MingchaoQi/skill_transfer.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Deploying robots in open-world environments involves managing complex operational scenarios, particularly when dealing with long sequences and contact-rich manipulation tasks \cite{lee2021ikea , \cite{heo2023furniturebench , \cite{lin2024generalize .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The diversity of these scenarios makes it impractical to program specific responses for each situation, necessitating a high degree of skill generalization.
elements.sent-2411-013: This ensures that robots can efficiently transfer learned skills to similar new scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-014: For instance, tasks such as opening doors and drawers require fundamental skills like approaching, grasping, pulling, and releasing, despite differences in objects and motion patterns.
elements.sent-2411-015: In these contexts, robots must flexibly transfer and integrate these skills.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=7.6cm]{experiment_setup.pdf} \caption{Skill Transfer for Manipulation: The task for the KUKA robotinvolves transferring the skill of opening a drawer to retrieve and stack cups to the task of opening a door to retrieve and stack cups in various orientations.} \label{experiment_setup}
elements.sent-2411-016: A prevalent method for skill transfer in robotic platforms is Learning from Demonstration (LfD), which enables robots to learn strategies from human or heuristic demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-017: For long-horizon tasks, robots must plan, execute, and sequence a set of interconnected actions over an extended period to achieve a final goal.
elements.sent-2411-018: Traditional LfD methods, such as Behavioral Cloning (BC), often perform poorly in these scenarios due to their inability to transfer the advanced skills necessary for completing complex tasks \cite{lin2024generalize .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Recent advances in end-to-end robot learning \cite{end_to_end_1 , \cite{end_to_end_2 , \cite{end_to_end_3 , \cite{end_to_end_4 have developed potent multimodal strategies that exhibit better generalization for tasks involving the same skill sequences.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, these methods require vast amounts of data, posing significant challenges for scalability in robotics.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=17cm]{structure.pdf} \caption{Our skill transfer method consists of two processes: the construction of a skill library and the hierarchical transfer of operational skills. Initially, the skill library encapsulates task and scenario semantic information using ``task graph" and ``scenario graphs", facilitating interaction via "state graph" through the ``require" and ``obtain" mechanisms. For hierarchical transfer, at the task level, a four-stage prompt system based on LLM is integrated with the skill library to accomplish sub-task sequence transfer. At the motion level, trajectory adaptive transfer is enabled through the use of "scenario graphs" and the A* algorithm. At the physical level, an adaptive method based on tactile perception is introduced to dynamically adjust contact positions and postures via contour extraction and posture perception, thereby enhancing the effectiveness of skill transfer.} \label{structure}
elements.sent-2411-021: The second type is a hierarchical design incorporating skill primitives and task planning.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this framework, each task is broken down into a hierarchy of skills managed by specific scripts or learning controllers, and these skills are stored in a constructed robot skill library for reuse in new tasks \cite{mandlekar2020learning , \cite{yang2021hierarchical , \cite{li2020generalized .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, it often prioritizes the combination of high-level skills while neglecting object properties and low-level contact issues, leading to difficulties in generalizing skills for contact-rich tasks.
elements.sent-2411-024: Tactile sensors like Gelsight \cite{yuan2017gelsight offer high-resolution tactile information, which can be utilized for precise estimation of object contours, textures, poses, forces, and torques, thereby supporting manipulation tasks \cite{lee2019touching .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Nonetheless, existing methods frequently rely on complete symbolic labels of objects or prior knowledge, such as known shapes, which makes it challenging to ensure robustness in diverse scenarios \cite{dragiev2013uncertainty .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We present the following contributions, as illustrated in the Figure~\ref{structure :.}
elements.sent-2411-027: This approach represents complex operational tasks hierarchically at both the task and action levels, linking them with spatial semantic information to facilitate hierarchical skill transfer.
elements.sent-2411-028: Utilizing the skill library, the framework enables task-level subtask sequence transfer, motion-level trajectory adaptive transfer, and physical-level tactile perception-driven adaptive transfer.
elements.sent-2411-029: This approach aims to facilitate the transfer of entire complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-030: This method dynamically acquires high-precision contour and pose information from texture data extracted from visuo-tactile images.
elements.sent-2411-031: It also internally adapts the transferred skills to ensure their effectiveness in new environments.
elements.sent-2411-032: A robot skill library is a structured set of skills, each corresponding to a specific task's operational process.
elements.sent-2411-033: These libraries are built through Imitation Learning and Reinforcement Learning, aimed at enabling skill reuse and composition for improved multi-tasking.
elements.sent-2411-034: Earlier robotic systems were task-specific with limited generalization.
elements.sent-2411-035: To improve adaptability, researchers proposed generalized skill libraries using modular skills for task planning \cite{kroemer2021review .}
elements.sent-2411-036: These libraries include basic and complex skills can be categorized as task-specific or general \cite{rozo2013robot ,\cite{rahmatizadeh2018vision .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Skills are often represented using behavior trees or finite state machines \cite{colledanchise2018behavior , and transfer learning allows robots to apply skills to new tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Skill transfer reduces training time and sample requirements by transferring knowledge from a source to a target domain.
elements.sent-2411-039: Key approaches include transfer learning, multitask learning, and meta-learning.
elements.sent-2411-040: Sun et al. \cite{achituve2021self proposed a self-supervised method for robots to adapt to dynamic environments.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Yu et al. \cite{yu2018learning demonstrated skill transfer through similar task examples, improving a robot's object grasping ability.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Multitask learning boosts efficiency across tasks by sharing representations \cite{du2018direct .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Meta-learning, like MAML, helps robots adapt quickly with few samples \cite{finn2017model .}
elements.sent-2411-044: RL with transfer learning, such as Progressive Neural Networks (PNNs) by Rusu et al. \cite{rusu2016progressive , shows strong skill transfer in complex tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Li et al. developed a method for precise widget positioning, enabling accurate USB connector placement using advanced matching techniques \cite{Tactile_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Izatt et al. enhanced this by integrating a filtering algorithm that merges vision and tactile data for object pose tracking, enabling precise tool operations \cite{Tactile_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Both used model-based tracking with predefined geometries.
elements.sent-2411-048: Daolin Ma et al. addressed locating contact points on unknown objects by solving for constraints \cite{Tactile_3 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Yu She et al. applied thresholding and PCA to deduce cable orientation from depth maps \cite{Tactile_4 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Achu Wilson et al. combined tactile feedback with vision for cable routing \cite{Tactile_5 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: This paper proposes a knowledge graph-based skill library framework designed to enable robots to comprehend high-level skills and spatial semantic information in scenes, facilitating the transfer of robot skills across similar scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-052: The skill library is depicted using a formalized semantic model knowledge graph (KG) \cite{KG_1 , \cite{KG_2 , \cite{KG_3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: This framework hierarchically structures knowledge by decomposing complex long-sequence tasks into sequences of subtasks, resulting in a "task graph" for robot operations.
elements.sent-2411-054: To effectively represent operational object information and physical constraints, this paper introduces a "scene graph" to depict spatial semantic information and employs a "state graph" to enable the interaction between operational tasks and scenes.
elements.sent-2411-055: The knowledge graph of the skill library is implemented using Neo4j.
elements.sent-2411-056: The Construction of Operational Task Graph.
elements.sent-2411-057: A complex task can be represented as a collection of subtasks $T = {T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n}$, where each subtask $T_i$ is a triple $(S_i, V_i, O_i)$ consisting of a subject $S_i$, predicate $V_i$, and object $O_i$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The predicate $V_i$ is mapped to a sequence of robot-executable action primitives through a function $f: V_i \to A^*$, such that $f(V_i) = (A_{i1 , A_{i2 , \dots, A_{ik )$, where each action primitive $A_{ij}$ corresponds to a low-level robot control command.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Based on the aforementioned theory, this paper decomposes a complex long-horizon task into two distinct levels: the task level and the action level.
elements.sent-2411-060: The task level includes the complex task $T$ and a sequence of subtasks $\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ derived through planning, where subtasks are considered as skills or high-level actions.
elements.sent-2411-061: The action level consists of action primitives, where a sequence of action primitives $\{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$ accomplishes a subtask.
elements.sent-2411-062: Action primitives refer to low-level, short-term tasks that can be directly executed through motion planning \cite{zhong2022skill .}
elements.sent-2411-063: To represent robotic operational skills, this paper constructs a knowledge graph $G = (V, E)$ to model the hierarchical relationships among tasks, subtasks, and action primitives.
elements.sent-2411-064: In this graph, nodes $V$ represent tasks, subtasks, and action primitives, while edges $E$ illustrate the relationships between them, forming an operational "task graph".
elements.sent-2411-065: The hierarchical node relationships include ``contain", ``next step", ``start" and ``end".
elements.sent-2411-066: The Construction of Operational Scene Graph.
elements.sent-2411-067: A scene graph can be represented as a directed graph \( G = \{O, E\} \), where \( O = \{O_1, O_2, \dots, O_n\} \) denotes the set of objects in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-068: Each object \( O_i = \{O_i, B_i, A_i\} \) consists of an object category \( O_i \), a position \( B_i \), and a set of attributes \( A_i \).
elements.sent-2411-069: The set of directed edges \( E = \{E_1, E_2, \dots, E_m\} \) represents relationships, with each edge \( E_k = \{O_i, r_{i \to j , O_j\} \) being an object-relationship-object triplet, indicating a relationship \( r_{i \to j \) between objects \( O_i \) and \( O_j \).}}
elements.sent-2411-070: For robots to effectively manage skill transfer tasks, it is essential to consider not only their task planning and action execution strategies but also the spatial semantic information of their environment.
elements.sent-2411-071: To address this, this paper introduces the concept of a "scene graph," which represents the structural composition of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-072: In this graph, each component and its configuration are modeled as subgraphs, allowing the robot to selectively focus on relevant parts while disregarding irrelevant information.
elements.sent-2411-073: The drawer box and the main body can be represented as two objects, $O_{\text{drawer }$ and $O_{\text{body }$, connected by a prismatic joint, represented as the directed edge $E_k = \{O_{\text{drawer , r_{\text{slider , O_{\text{body }\}$ where $r_{\text{slider }$ denotes the sliding relationship.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The motion range of the joint, damping, friction, and other physical properties are represented as attributes $A_i$, while the dimensions and relative positions of the objects are summarized as attributes $B_i$.
elements.sent-2411-075: Using this approach, a scene graph containing physical information can be constructed and incorporated into a knowledge graph using Neo4j.
elements.sent-2411-076: This method allows the creation of a comprehensive scene graph that includes operational spatial semantic information, which can then be transformed into a knowledge graph using Neo4j.
elements.sent-2411-077: The Construction of Operational State Graph.
elements.sent-2411-078: The ``task graph" and ``scene graph" outlined earlier are mutually isolated.
elements.sent-2411-079: To resolve this issue, this paper introduces the concept of a ``state graph", which integrates the ``task graph" and ``scene graph", thus forming a comprehensive knowledge graph for the robot operation skill library.
elements.sent-2411-080: Essentially, this process involves linking the subtask sequences in the task graph with the entity relationship information in the scene graph.
elements.sent-2411-081: To achieve this connection, the concepts of ``Require" and ``Obtain" are introduced during the robot's subtask sequence execution.
elements.subsection-2411-005: Hierarchical Transfer Framework of Operational Skills
elements.sent-2411-082: The primary design of this framework is based on the previously constructed skill library knowledge graph.
elements.sent-2411-083: It implements task-level subtask sequence transfer, motion-level trajectory adaptive transfer, and operation adaptive transfer driven by physical-level tactile perception.
elements.sent-2411-085: Subtask sequences planned in similar task scenarios are always akin, such as in the tasks of opening doors and drawers.
elements.sent-2411-086: To enable the transfer of task-level subtask sequences across similar scenarios, it is essential for the agent to have a comprehensive understanding of the transfer tasks and scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-087: In this context, we introduce LLM, leveraging its powerful reasoning and generalization capabilities to achieve the aforementioned goals.
elements.sent-2411-088: This paper is based on the contextual learning and thought-chain prompts in prompt-based learning, constructing a new four-stage prompt word paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-089: First, we provide the LLM with a comprehensive description of the complex planning task and the state graph descriptions from the skill library.
elements.sent-2411-090: This includes detailed accounts of long-horizon operations tasks, their subtask sequences, and the meanings and specific components associated with ``Require" and ``Obtain" in the state graphs.
elements.sent-2411-091: Next, we enable the LLM to comprehend the entire knowledge graph of the operations skill library.
elements.sent-2411-092: We input the skill library into the LLM as Neo4j-based code, which helps the model understand subtask sequences in the task graph, components of objects in the scene graph, their connections, and their relative positional relationships.
elements.sent-2411-093: In the third step, we guide the LLM to deeply understand the spatial semantic information in the scene graph of the skill library.
elements.sent-2411-094: Despite the initial stages, the LLM may still have deficiencies in understanding this complex spatial semantic information—such as relationships between objects and the connections between object components.
elements.sent-2411-095: To address these issues, we customize a specific form of expression: ``object - connection type - object" for relationships.
elements.sent-2411-096: Finally, we provide reference planning for existing complex tasks and subtask sequence migration requirements for new scenes.
elements.sent-2411-097: In this step, constraints and regulations noted in the ``notes" are crucial to ensure that our subtask sequence migration remains within specified limits and does not generate redundant information.
elements.sent-2411-098: Consequently, the LLM can achieve complex task planning from the existing scene to the new scene and associate planned subtasks with spatial semantic information in the new scene, aligning with the state graph information corresponding to the subtasks.
elements.sent-2411-100: The transferred subtask sequences, represented in human semantic knowledge, cannot be directly translated into action primitives executable by robots.
elements.sent-2411-101: Therefore, transferring motion-level trajectories becomes necessary.
elements.sent-2411-102: Direct trajectory transfer can face challenges, especially in tasks like opening doors and drawers, where the mechanisms differ significantly—drawers require pushing or pulling along a translational axis, whereas doors operate around a rotational axis.
elements.sent-2411-103: Deviations from the ideal trajectory by the robot can exert excessive lateral forces, potentially causing damage.
elements.sent-2411-104: Additionally, changes in obstacles along the path during door or drawer operations may lead to collisions.
elements.sent-2411-105: We propose an adaptive trajectory transfer method leveraging the A* algorithm and a robotic operation skill library to achieve motion-level trajectory adaptation for robot skills.
elements.sent-2411-106: This method initially employs the skill library’s ``scene graph'' to retrieve spatial semantic information and generate a corresponding three-dimensional discretized model.
elements.sent-2411-107: Subsequently, it identifies the trajectory's start and end points based on the new task's subtask sequence and ``state graph" information, which is generated in an earlier phase with the assistance of a LLM.
elements.sent-2411-108: Finally, the A* algorithm autonomously plans the trajectory and avoidance tasks using task and scene information.
elements.sent-2411-109: This approach enables the robot's operational trajectory to adapt to various tasks, ensuring smooth and safe task completion in new environments.
elements.sent-2411-110: Physical-level tactile perception-driven adaptive manipulation transfer.
elements.sent-2411-111: Task-level and motion-level transfers facilitate the generalization of specific tasks in new environments.
elements.sent-2411-112: However, for tasks involving intricate tactile interactions, robots must manage the variability of object attributes and the complexity of underlying contact mechanisms, which constrains the adaptability and generalization of their skills.
elements.sent-2411-113: This paper introduces an adaptive learning mechanism that dynamically adjusts operational strategies based on real-time feedback from high-resolution visuotactile sensors such as Gelsight.
elements.sent-2411-114: The feedback encompasses information about an object's contours, textures, posture, forces, and torques.
elements.sent-2411-115: For example, when grasping objects with varying textures, robots can modify their force and grip angle in response to perceived hardness or slip characteristics, thereby maintaining stability and precision under different contact conditions and adapting to diverse operational environments.
elements.sent-2411-116: However, existing tactile perception methods struggle to maintain robustness across diverse scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-117: To achieve physical-level tactile perception-driven operational adaptive transfer, this study proposes an adaptive method for contour extraction and pose estimation based on tactile perception, which will be introduced in the following section.
elements.subsection-2411-006: Haptic Contour Extraction Algorithm Based on Image Texture
elements.sent-2411-118: To accurately distinguish contours in tactile images, it is necessary to divide the contact area into three regions: texture region, contour region, and non-contact region.
elements.sent-2411-119: The contour region is characterized by sharp gradient changes; the non-contact region primarily exhibits scattered, low-gradient noise; and the texture region is determined by the surface and contact conditions of the touched object, typically displaying regular patterns with moderate gradient strength.
elements.sent-2411-120: The algorithm first defines the texture threshold ($T_texture$) as the floor value of the average of non-zero gradient values in the gradient map \(G\), given by the following equation: \begin{equation}T_texture = \left\lfloor \frac{1}{|G|} \sum_{i,j: G_{i,j} \neq 0} G_{i,j} \right\rfloor \label{eq:texture}\end{equation} Next, each pixel \((i, j)\) in the image \(I\) is smoothed by applying a Gaussian kernel \(G_k\), resulting in the smoothed image \(S\).
elements.sent-2411-121: Then, the gradient magnitude \(G_{i,j}\) and gradient direction \(\Theta_{i,j}\) are computed for each pixel.
elements.sent-2411-122: Non-maximum suppression is applied to retain local maximum gradient values, refining the contour points.
elements.sent-2411-123: A dual-threshold detection is then performed on these contour points using dynamically adjusted high and low thresholds (\(T_{\text{high }\) and \(T_{\text{low }\)), optimized to match the texture information.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Through this process, the final set of contour points \(C\) is determined.
elements.sent-2411-125: Finally, the Hough Transform is used to extract lines from the contour point set and compute the line direction vectors \(H\), providing the necessary data for further processing or analysis.
elements.sent-2411-126: The haptic contour extraction algorithm based on image texture is designed to process a 320x240 resolution Gelsight image and extract contour point sets and line direction vectors using the Hough transform.
elements.sent-2411-127: The complete processing flow and pseudocode are presented in Figure~\ref{tactile_perception and Algorithm~\ref{algorithm , respectively.}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=6.5cm]{tactile_perception.pdf} \caption{Adaptive contour extraction process diagram. The blue scatter represents the extracted edges, while the green line indicates the direction obtained from the Hough transform } \label{tactile_perception}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Haptic Contour Extraction Algorithm Based on Image Texture} \label{alg:AOS} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: A 320x240 Gelsight image $I$ \State Output: Contour point set $C$ and line direction vectors obtained by Hough transform $H$ \Procedure{ContourExtraction}{} \State $T_texture$ $\gets$ \{$G$\} with equation \ref{eq:texture} \For{each pixel $(i, j)$ in $I$} \State $S_{i,j} \gets$ smooth $I$ with $G_k$ \State $G_{i,j} = \sqrt{(S_{i+1,j} - S_{i-1,j})^2 + (S_{i,j+1} - S_{i,j-1})^2}$ \State $\Theta_{i,j} = \arctan\left(\frac{S_{i,j+1} - S_{i,j-1}}{S_{i+1,j} - S_{i-1,j}}\right)$ \EndFor \For{each pixel $(i, j)$ in $G$} \State $N_{i,j} \gets$ \{$G$, $\Theta$\} non-maximum suppression \State $C_{i,j} \gets$ \{$G$, $T_texture$\} dual threshold detection \EndFor \State $T_{\text{high}}, T_{\text{low}} \gets$ adjust with $T_texture$ \State $H \gets$ \{C\} with Hough Transform \EndProcedure \State Return: $C$ and $H$ \end{algorithmic} \label{algorithm}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=12cm]{skill_transfer_experiment.pdf} \caption{Skill Transfer Result Diagram. The first row illustrates the initial skills, while the second row depicts the process of effectively completing tasks after skill transfer. During this process, tactile feedback is integrated into the control loop.} \label{skill_transfer_experiment}
elements.sent-2411-128: In this paper, the robotic arm utilized is the KUKA LBR iiwa 14 R820.
elements.sent-2411-129: The tactile sensor employed is the Gelsight mini, with a sampling frequency of 10Hz.
elements.sent-2411-130: For low-level control, we use position control in the end-effector's Cartesian space at a frequency of 100 Hz, enabling the generation of smooth interpolated trajectories.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Validation of the Effectiveness of the Hierarchical Skill Transfer Framework
elements.sent-2411-131: In our experiments, we set up a drawer and a cabinet with doors containing vertically striped mugs in various positions.
elements.sent-2411-132: We aimed to transfer the skills of opening a drawer and retrieving a mug to a designated stacking location to the task of opening a cabinet door and retrieving mugs in different positions for stacking.
elements.sent-2411-133: In this transfer scenario, the robot is required to complete the transfer of a long sub-task sequence, which includes approaching, grasping, pulling, and releasing the handles of drawers or doors, followed by approaching, picking, and moving to the designated location to accurately stack mugs.
elements.sent-2411-134: Throughout the movement process, the robot must adaptively complete the trajectory planning tasks based on the scenario information provided by the skill library.
elements.sent-2411-135: During the mug stacking phase, the robot adjusts its orientation adaptively, utilizing the tactile representation of physical-level information such as the mug’s contour and orientation, to successfully complete the stacking.
elements.sent-2411-136: As illustrated in the Figure~\ref{skill_transfer_experiment , the experimental results demonstrate that the robot successfully completed the aforementioned tasks using the hierarchical skill transfer framework described in this paper, confirming the framework's effectiveness in handling complex manipulative tasks with long sequences and rich contact interactions.}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Evaluation of the Skill Transfer Framework
elements.sent-2411-137: When addressing new operating scenarios, reinforcement learning (RL) methods are frequently employed for policy exploration.
elements.sent-2411-138: However, the training process demands extensive interaction with the environment, which is often inefficient and poses safety risks.
elements.sent-2411-139: Consequently, this paper focuses on a specific aspect of skill transfer: transferring the drawer-opening skill to the door-opening task.
elements.sent-2411-140: Experiments were conducted within the simulated environment, Robosuite.
elements.sent-2411-141: To evaluate the efficiency of the hierarchical skill transfer method proposed in this paper, we implemented the hierarchical transfer framework for operational skills in a simulated environment and compared its performance with that of the direct policy transfer method.
elements.sent-2411-142: The direct policy transfer approach involves pre-training with the SAC reinforcement learning algorithm in a drawer-opening environment, followed by the direct transfer of the policy network learned by the SAC algorithm to an untrained door-opening task to observe the performance of the transferred policy in the new task.The design of the reward function for the drawer-opening task is as follows: \begin{equation}\text{r} = \begin{cases} 2 \text{success} 0.25 \times \left( 1 - \tanh(10 \times \text{d}) \right) \text{unsuccess} \end{cases}\end{equation} where $success$ and $unsuccess$ indicate whether the task of opening the drawer is completed, and $d$ represents the Euclidean distance between the robot gripper and the target position on the drawer handle.
elements.sent-2411-143: Train the drawer-opening algorithm using the SAC algorithm from Stable Baselines3 for 1000 episodes, each with a fixed length of 1000 time steps.
elements.sent-2411-144: The training results are presented in the Figure~\ref{open_drawer .}
elements.sent-2411-145: Then, transfer the trained policy network to the door-opening task and test its performance over 50 episodes, each consisting of 1000 steps.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=6cm]{open_drawer.png} \caption{Results of training the SAC algorithm on the drawer-opening task in the Robosuite simulation environment. } \label{open_drawer}
elements.sent-2411-147: The results indicate that the success rate of the direct transfer strategy is 0\% within 50 episodes, while our method achieves a 100\% success rate within the same timeframe.
elements.sent-2411-148: This suggests that our approach is more effective and robust when applied to various environments and tasks.
elements.subsection-2411-010: Verification of Adaptive Tactile Threshold Algorithm
elements.sent-2411-149: The goal of this experiment is to evaluate the performance of a tactile-based adaptive contour extraction algorithm in various scenarios, with a focus on the accuracy and reliability of extracting six typical contours.
elements.sent-2411-150: To achieve this, we employ our adaptive method and compare its performance with a fixed-threshold edge extraction method.
elements.sent-2411-151: During the experiment, multiple data collection sessions were conducted for each sample at a sampling rate of 10 Hz, with each session lasting approximately 5 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-152: To assess the differences in performance between the adaptive contour extraction algorithm and the fixed-threshold method, we utilized Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) to measure the positional error between the extracted contours and the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-153: A lower RMSE indicates better algorithm performance.Figure~\ref{fig:experiment_3 shows the results of our algorithm intuitively and Table~\ref{tab:rmse-errors summarizes the RMSE positional and angular errors under different conditions for both algorithms.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: The experimental data demonstrate that the adaptive contour extraction algorithm outperforms the fixed-threshold method when handling various geometric shapes and complex surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-155: Notably, the adaptive algorithm exhibits stronger robustness in scenarios with multiple edges (complex geometry) or contours located at the edge of the sensor’s field of view (challenging contact conditions).
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=7.6cm]{experiment_3.pdf} \caption{Tactile adaptive contour extraction results. The blue scatter points represent the extracted contour points, while the green lines represent the straight edges perceived through the Hough line transform applied to the contour points.} \label{fig:experiment_3}
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{RMSE of Tactile Adaptive and Fixed Thresholds on Various Geometrical Shapes.} \fontsize{9pt}{11pt}\selectfont \begin{tabular}{l>{ \arraybackslash}p{1.1cm} >{ \arraybackslash}p{1.1cm} >{ \arraybackslash}p{1.1cm} >{ \arraybackslash}p{1.1cm}} Rect Acute Right Circle Fixed Threshold 39.31 101.76 112.81 326.04 Tactile Adaptive 37.15 61.52 62.38 63.80 \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{l>{ \arraybackslash}p{1.1cm} >{ \arraybackslash}p{1.1cm} >{ \arraybackslash}p{1.1cm} >{ \arraybackslash}p{1.1cm}} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Pentagon} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Line Bundle} \cmidrule(lr){2-3} \cmidrule(lr){4-5} General Edge General Edge Fixed Threshold 123.30 203.59 117.44 214.16 Tactile Adaptive 61.87 102.05 103.03 193.92 \end{tabular} [5pt] 0.5\textwidth{ Rect, acute, right represent straight line, acute angle, right angle respectively. A smaller numerical value indicates better performance.} \label{tab:rmse-errors}
elements.sent-2411-156: This paper introduces a method designed to create a skill library and a hierarchical skill transfer framework, which facilitates the transfer of robotic skills in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-157: It employs ``task graph" and ``scene graph" to delineate task and scene information respectively, and uses ``state graph" for interactive purposes.
elements.sent-2411-158: This approach enables the transfer of subtask sequences at the task level, adaptive trajectory transfer at the motion level, and tactile-based skill adjustments at the physical level.
elements.sent-2411-159: The method's adaptability in complex environments has been confirmed through experimental verification.. \bibliographystyle{BibTex/IEEEtran \.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Developing general robotic systems capable of manipulating in unstructured environments is a significant challenge, particularly as the tasks involved are typically long-horizon and rich-contact, requiring efficient skill transfer across different task scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-161: To address these challenges, we propose knowledge graph-based skill library construction method.
elements.sent-2411-162: This method hierarchically organizes manipulation knowledge using ``task graph'' and ``scene graph'' to represent task-specific and scene-specific information, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-163: Additionally, we introduce ``state graph'' to facilitate the interaction between high-level task planning and low-level scene information.
elements.sent-2411-164: Building upon this foundation, we further propose a novel hierarchical skill transfer framework based on the skill library and tactile representation, which integrates high-level reasoning for skill transfer and low-level precision for execution.
elements.sent-2411-165: At the task level, we utilize large language models (LLMs) and combine contextual learning with a four-stage chain-of-thought prompting paradigm to achieve subtask sequence transfer.
elements.sent-2411-166: At the motion level, we develop an adaptive trajectory transfer method based on the skill library and the heuristic path planning algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-167: At the physical level, we propose an adaptive contour extraction and posture perception method based on tactile representation.
elements.sent-2411-168: This method dynamically acquires high-precision contour and posture information from visual-tactile images, adjusting parameters such as contact position and posture to ensure the effectiveness of transferred skills in new environments.
elements.sent-2411-169: Experiments demonstrate the skill transfer and adaptability capabilities of the proposed methods across different task scenarios.. Project website: \url{https://github.com/MingchaoQi/skill_transfer.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Building a universal robotic manipulating system has always been a challenging task, mainly due to the complexity and variability of the real world \cite{huang2024rekep, wu2023learning, wu2024canonical .}
elements.sent-2411-171: Especially when faced with long-horizon, contact-rich complex tasks, it is difficult to program the robot for each specific scenario \cite{heo2023furniturebench .}
elements.sent-2411-172: A promising solution is to enable the skills learned by robots in previous scenarios to have good generalization, allowing efficient transfer to similar new scenarios \cite{pan2025omnimanip .}
elements.sent-2411-173: For example, tasks such as opening various types of drawers or cabinets to retrieve and place objects, or assembling different parts in a factory in distinct ways.
elements.sent-2411-174: In these scenarios, a similar set of robot skill sequences is required.
elements.sent-2411-175: However, it is important to note that the objects involved and the motion types may differ.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{experiment_setup.pdf} \caption{Skill Transfer for Manipulation. Transferring skills learned from previous scenarios to new contexts is essential. For example, the skill of opening a drawer to retrieve and stack cups should be transferred to the new task of opening a door to retrieve cups in different orientations and complete the stacking.} \label{experiment_setup}
elements.sent-2411-176: One way to enhance manipulation generalization is through imitation learning, where large-scale robot datasets are used to pre-train Vision-Language Models (VLM) to construct Vision-Language Action Models (VLA) \cite{brohan2022rt , \cite{duan2024manipulate .}}
elements.sent-2411-177: These can learn strong multimodal policies, but obtaining diverse, high-quality robotic data is costly and time-consuming, and the method’s generalization capability remains limited when handling new scenes, especially with unseen object states.
elements.sent-2411-178: An alternative way to enhance manipulation generalization is to apply the world knowledge embedded in Large Language Models (LLM) to policy learning \cite{tang2023graspgpt , \cite{huang2024copa , reducing data dependency.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: Some research utilizes LLM’s reasoning ability to predict reasonable action sequences and parse environmental information into text representations \cite{mirchandani2023large , \cite{mandi2024roco .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: However, since these methods rely on coarse-grained environmental representations, they only manage simple tasks like pick-and-place and object rearrangement.
elements.sent-2411-181: Particularly for long-horizon tasks, LLMs struggle to rationally plan fully executable action sequences.
elements.sent-2411-182: Moreover, these methods often focus on the combination of high-level skills while neglecting the properties of manipulation objects and underlying contact issues, limiting skill generalization in contact-rich tasks.
elements.sent-2411-183: To address these issues, we propose a novel hierarchical skill transfer framework that integrates skill library with fine tactile representation, thereby combining high-level reasoning with low-level precise execution capabilities to efficiently transfer skills across long-horizon, contact-rich scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-184: Specifically, we first construct a skill library based on knowledge graph, using ``task graph'' and ``scene graph'' to represent task and scene information, respectively, and through ``state graph,'' complete the interaction between high-level task information and low-level precise physical information.
elements.sent-2411-185: On this basis, we build the hierarchical skill transfer framework, which divide the entire skill transfer process into three layers from high to low.
elements.sent-2411-186: At the task layer, we use LLMs, inputting the skill library in code form, then implement subtask sequence transfer through a four-stage prompting paradigm combining in-context learning and chain-of-thought prompting.
elements.sent-2411-187: At the motion layer, with the aid of precise spatial scene physical information from the skill library, we use the heuristic A* path planning algorithm \cite{xiangrong2021improved to achieve adaptive trajectory transfer.}
elements.sent-2411-188: Finally, at the physical layer, we propose an adaptive contour extraction and posture perception method based on tactile representation \cite{xu2024unit , dynamically acquiring high precision contour and posture information through visual-tactile images, adjusting contact position and posture parameters to ensure the efficiency of skill transfer in contact-rich new environments.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-190: A hierarchical skill transfer framework based on skill library and tactile representation is proposed, incorporating high-level reasoning and low-level precise execution to efficiently adapt existing skills to new scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-191: A knowledge graph-based skill library construction method is proposed, which represents task and scene information through ``task graph'' and ``scene graph'' and utilizes ``state graph'' to facilitate their interaction.
elements.sent-2411-192: A tactile perception-based adaptive contour extraction and pose estimation method is proposed, enabling internal self-adaptation of transferred skills at the physical level to ensure their effectiveness in new scenes.
elements.sent-2411-193: A robot skill library is a structured set of skills, each corresponding to a specific task’s manipulation process.
elements.sent-2411-194: These libraries include basic and complex skills and can be categorized as task-specific or general \cite{rozo2013robot .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Li et al. \cite{li2014localization achieved precise tracking and positioning of small widgets through high-precision matching and map analysis, successfully locating and inserting USB connectors.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Izatt et al. \cite{izatt2017tracking extended this approach by proposing a filtering algorithm that fuses vision and tactile information to track object poses accurately, enabling precise grasping and insertion operations for a screwdriver.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Both studies assumed known object geometries and focused on model-based tracking.
elements.sent-2411-198: Daolin Ma et al. \cite{ma2021extrinsic addressed the challenge of locating contact points with unknown objects and environments by directly solving constraint problems to estimate the contact positions.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Yu She et al. \cite{she2021cable used thresholding and PCA to extract the principal components of the contact area depth map, determining cable orientation for manipulation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Achu Wilson et al. \cite{wilson2023cable combined tactile-guided low-level motion control with vision-based task parsing to achieve cable routing and assembly tasks.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Overview_v2.pdf} \caption{Overview Framework. We first propose the skill library based on knowledge graph, representing high-level tasks and low-level scene information through ``task graph'' and ``scene graph''. The ``state graph'' facilitates interaction between them via ``require'' and ``obtain'' mechanisms. Based on this, we propose hierarchical skill transfer framework. At the task level, we employ a four-stage prompting paradigm based on large language models and the skill library to achieve subtask sequence transfer. At the motion level, we retrieve relevant physical information from the ``scene graph'' and utilize heuristic path planning algorithms to achieve adaptive trajectory transfer. At the physical level, we introduce a tactile perception-based adaptive posture adjustment method, dynamically modifying contact positions and postures through contour extraction and pose sensing.} \label{structure}
elements.sent-2411-201: Our proposed method consists of two parts: Skill Library constructed based on knowledge graph and Hierarchical Skill Transfer Framework, as shown in Figure~\ref{structure .}
elements.sent-2411-202: Here we will discuss in detail: (1) How to construct robotic manipulation skill library based on the knowledge graph (Sec.
elements.sent-2411-203: III-A)? (2) How to achieve hierarchical skill transfer based on the skill library and tactile representation (Sec.
elements.sent-2411-204: III-B)? (3) How is tactile representation implemented and how does it function in physical layer transfer (Sec.. III-C)?.
elements.sent-2411-205: To enable robots to understand high-level semantic skills and detailed scene information at the physical level, we constructed skill library based on knowledge graph(KG) \cite{daruna2019robocse .}
elements.sent-2411-206: Specifically, the skill library consists of three parts: the ``Task Graph,'' which hierarchically constructs knowledge by decomposing long-horizon tasks into sub-task sequences; the ``Scene Graph,'' which is built to effectively describe precise physical information and spatial constraints of manipulation objects; and the ``State Graph,'' which is constructed to enable the interaction between the former two.
elements.sent-2411-207: In this section, we detail how each of these three components is constructed and how they interact to form a complete skill library.
elements.sent-2411-208: The Construction of Manipulation Task Graph.
elements.sent-2411-209: As shown in Figure~\ref{structure (c), given a long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation task $T$ (e.g., retrieving a cup from drawer and stacking), we first decompose the task into multiple stages, forming a sub-task sequence $S = \{S_1, S_2, ..., S_n\}$, where each stage $S_i$ can be formalized as $S_i = \{A_i, O_i^{\text{actor , O_i^{\text{target }\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-210: In this, $A_i$ represents the action to be performed (e.g., grasping, opening drawer), $O_i^{\text{actor }$ denotes the object initiating the action $A_i$, and $O_i^{\text{target }$ denotes the object that the action $A_i$ is aimed at.}}
elements.sent-2411-211: For example, for the ``stacking'' action, the cup grasped by the gripper is the actor object, while the cup placed on the table is the target object.
elements.sent-2411-212: Based on this definition, we decompose long-horizon tasks into two hierarchical levels: the task level and the motion level.
elements.sent-2411-213: The task level comprises the high-level manipulation task $T$ and the planned sequence of subtasks $S = \{S_1, S_2, ..., S_n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-214: The action level consists of an action primitive sequence $\{A_1, A_2, ..., A_n\}$, where action primitives are low-level, short-term actions that can be directly executed by robots through motion planning \cite{xiangrong2021improved .}
elements.sent-2411-215: This hierarchical design distinguishes high-level semantic information from low-level executable actions, providing a foundation for hierarchical skill transfer in robotics.
elements.sent-2411-216: Specifically, we utilize Neo4j to construct ``Task Graph'' $G^T = (V^T, E^T)$, where subtasks at the task level and action primitives at the motion level are represented as nodes $V^T$ in the graph, and the relationships between these nodes are represented as edges $E^T$.
elements.sent-2411-217: Constructing the knowledge graph with Neo4j allows each node $V^T$ and edge $E^T$ to have their own properties.
elements.sent-2411-218: We incorporate $O_i^{\text{actor }$ and $O_i^{\text{target }$ from $S_i$ as attributes of the corresponding node $V^T$.}}
elements.sent-2411-219: The properties of the edges $E^T$ represent different execution relationships within the task and action levels, such as ``start,'' ``next,'' and ``end''.
elements.sent-2411-220: Additionally, edges between the task and action levels encode ``contain'' relationship, indicating that a subtask $S$ may contain multiple sequentially executed actions $A_i$.
elements.sent-2411-221: The Construction of Manipulation Scene Graph.
elements.sent-2411-222: We represent the scene graph as \( G^S = (V^S, E^S) \), where \( V^S = \{v^S_1, v^S_2, \dots, v^S_n\} \) denotes the set of objects in the scene, and each object node \( v^S_i \) includes attributes such as category, position, and orientation.
elements.sent-2411-223: The edges \( E^S = \{e^S_1, e^S_2, \dots, e^S_m\} \) define the spatial or functional relationships between objects.
elements.sent-2411-224: As shown in Figure~\ref{structure (e), when constructing the scene graph to guide robot manipulation, we extend these concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Instead of modeling only entire objects as nodes $v_i^S$, represent the constituent parts of objects as nodes $v_i^S$.
elements.sent-2411-226: This is because robotic manipulation requires not only an understanding of an object's overall spatial properties but also its operable parts, such as the handle of a drawer or cabinet.
elements.sent-2411-227: Each node is assigned three attributes: category $c_i$, position $p_i$ (Cartesian coordinates), and orientation $q_i$ (quaternion).
elements.sent-2411-228: Consequently, the edges $e_i^S$ represent not only spatial relationships between objects (e.g., inside, beside) but also functional relationships between parts of an object (e.g., joint, attach).
elements.sent-2411-229: When an edge $e_i^S$ represents spatial relationship, its attributes describe the relative position and orientation between two objects.
elements.sent-2411-230: If $e_i^S$ represents a joint connection, additional joint attributes are included, as an object's movement constraints are crucial for robotic manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-231: These joint attributes are encoded using XML, a common language for spatial modeling.
elements.sent-2411-232: For example, as depicted in Figure~\ref{structure (e), the cabinet body and door are connected by a revolute joint, with the rotation axis defined as vertical (xyz=``0 0 1''), the joint location at the ``origin,'' and the connected parts labeled as ``child'' and ``parent.'' The Construction of Manipulation State Graph.}
elements.sent-2411-233: Although the ``task graph'' and ``scene graph'' are constructed, they remain independent of each other.
elements.sent-2411-234: To establish a connection between them, we introduce the concept of ``state graph,'' which associates the subtask sequence in the ``task graph'' with the entity relationships in the ``scene graph'', as illustrated in Figure~\ref{structure (d).}
elements.sent-2411-235: To enable their interaction during robot execution, we define the concepts of ``Require'' and ``Obtain.'' The ``Require'' mechanism specifies the necessary conditions for executing a given subtask $S_i$, including the initiating object $O_i^{\text{actor }$, the target object $O_i^{\text{target }$, and the required state properties.}}
elements.sent-2411-236: These properties can be extracted from the attributes of nodes $V^S$ and edges $E^S$ in the skill library knowledge graph.
elements.sent-2411-237: The ``Obtain'' mechanism captures the state changes resulting from the completion of $S_i$.
elements.sent-2411-238: These changes are then fed back into the ``scene graph'', updating the attributes of relevant nodes and edges, thereby providing information support for the subsequent subtask execution.
elements.sent-2411-239: This entire process can be implemented using Neo4j.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Hierarchical Transfer Framework of Manipulation Skills
elements.sent-2411-240: Our framework achieves hierarchical skill transfer based on skill library and tactile representation.
elements.sent-2411-241: Specifically, it sequentially implements task-level subtask sequence transfer, motion-level trajectory adaptive transfer, and physical-level manipulation adaptive transfer based on tactile representation.
elements.sent-2411-242: In summary, this framework enables skill transfer across different scenarios at the task level and achieves adaptive goal accomplishment strategies within skills at the motion and physical levels.
elements.sent-2411-244: In similar task scenarios, the subtask sequences required for planning and execution are often similar.
elements.sent-2411-245: For example, the robot skill sequence for opening a drawer and opening a door typically involves: approaching, grasping, pulling, and releasing.
elements.sent-2411-246: However, it is important to note that there are differences in the objects being manipulated and the modes of motion.
elements.sent-2411-247: To facilitate the transfer of subtask sequences between similar scenarios, the robot must fully understand both the transfer task and the transfer scenario.
elements.sent-2411-248: Therefore, we introduce the LLM, leveraging contextual learning and chain-of-thought prompting, to construct a new four-stage prompting paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-249: This approach utilizes the LLM's powerful reasoning and generalization capabilities to achieve this objective.
elements.sent-2411-250: First, we provide the LLM with a comprehensive description of the complex planning task and the ``state graph'' descriptions from the skill library.
elements.sent-2411-251: This includes detailed accounts of long-horizon tasks, their subtask sequences, and the meanings and specific components associated with ``Require'' and ``Obtain'' in the ``state graph''.
elements.sent-2411-252: In the third step, we guide the LLM to deeply understand the spatial semantic information within the scene graph.
elements.sent-2411-253: Due to the complexity of the scene graph, although the LLM can effectively analyze the attributes of various components within the scene graph after the first two steps, its understanding of spatial semantic information remains insufficient.
elements.sent-2411-254: For instance, it struggles to accurately output the spatial relationships of the components within objects.
elements.sent-2411-255: To address this, we build upon the second step by designing a specific triple template ``Object1-Connection Type-Object2'' to enhance the prompts.
elements.sent-2411-256: Here, ``Object'' represents objects or their components, while ``Connection Type'' corresponds to the spatial or functional relationship between the two objects.
elements.sent-2411-257: Finally, reference plan for known complex tasks and a description of similar tasks to be executed in the new scenario are provided.
elements.sent-2411-258: In this step, we impose constraints through ``note'' to ensure that the subtask sequence transfer remains within the defined boundaries, thus preventing the generation of redundant information.
elements.sent-2411-259: As a result, the LLMs outputs a task planning sequence $S = \{S_1, S_2, ..., S_n\}$ for the new scenario, where each $S_i$ corresponds to an action $A_i$.
elements.sent-2411-260: For each action $A_i$, the initiating object in the new scenario is $O_i^{\text{actor }$, and the target object is $O_i^{\text{target }$, represented as $\{A_i, O_i^{\text{actor , O_i^{\text{target }\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-262: After task-level transfer, the subtask sequence is represented using human semantic knowledge, which the robot cannot directly execute.
elements.sent-2411-263: Therefore, motion-level trajectory transfer is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-264: Different task scenarios involve distinct object motion patterns; for example, pulling a drawer requires linear motion along an axis, whereas opening a cabinet involves rotational motion around an axis.
elements.sent-2411-265: Additionally, obstacles may differ across scenarios, potentially leading to collisions.
elements.sent-2411-266: To address this, we propose an adaptive trajectory transfer method that utilizes heuristic A* algorithm-based trajectory planning in conjunction with skill library.
elements.sent-2411-267: Once interaction primitives and their corresponding spatial constraints are defined for each subtask, the subtask execution can be framed as an optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-268: For each action $A_i$ in the subtask sequence $S_i$, we first retrieve state information, including positions, orientations, and joint attributes of $O_i^{\text{actor }$ and $O_i^{\text{target }$, from the scene graph using the ``Require'' query based on task-level transfer results $\{A_i, O_i^{\text{actor , O_i^{\text{target }\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-269: Subsequently, the start and end points of action $A_i$'s trajectory are computed, and a corresponding 3D discretized map is generated.
elements.sent-2411-270: The A* algorithm is then applied for path planning.
elements.sent-2411-271: To prevent collisions between the end-effector and obstacles in the environment when operating in a new scenario, we define the collision loss $L_{\text{collision }$ as: \begin{equation}L_{\text{collision}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max(0, \delta - d(p_e^i, O_i))^2\end{equation} where $d(p_e^i, O_i)$ denotes the distance between the end-effector $p_e^i$ and the obstacle $O_i$, while $\delta$ represents the minimum allowable safety distance.}
elements.sent-2411-272: When applying the A* algorithm for path planning, minimizing collision loss ensures the successful execution of the action sequence $A_i$ corresponding to the subtask sequence $S_i$ in a new task environment.
elements.sent-2411-273: This strategy not only prevents collisions but also promotes smooth motion, facilitating the adaptive transfer of motion-level trajectories to the new environment.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=6.5cm]{tactile_perception.pdf} \caption{In Figure (a), \( q_2 \) and \( q_3 \) correspond to the sensor's mounting pose and the end-effector pose of the robotic arm, respectively, while Figures (a) and (b) illustrate the pose error \( q_e \) that needs to be adjusted and the target pose \( q_t \) to be reached, represented by the yellow and red dashed lines, respectively. In Figure (c), the pose information \( q_1 \) obtained through the algorithm is shown, with the blue scatter points indicating the extracted edges and the green lines representing the directions derived from the Hough transform. Ultimately, by performing calculations with the target pose, a pose control command for the robotic arm is generated.} \label{tactile_perception}
elements.sent-2411-274: Physical-level Transfer based on Tactile Representation.
elements.sent-2411-275: Task-level and motion-level transfers facilitate the generalization of specific tasks in new scenes.
elements.sent-2411-276: We introduce an adaptive learning mechanism that dynamically adjusts manipulation strategies based on real-time feedback from high-resolution visuotactile sensors such as Gelsight.
elements.sent-2411-277: To achieve physical-level tactile perception-driven adaptive transfer, we propose an adaptive method for contour extraction and pose estimation based on tactile perception, which will be introduced in the following section (Sec.. III-C).
elements.subsection-2411-012: Pose Adjustment Utilizing Tactile Contour Extraction
elements.sent-2411-278: In order to predict the pose of textured objects, we propose dividing the tactile image into three regions: texture region, contour region, and non-contact region.
elements.sent-2411-279: Pose information is then extracted from the texture region to facilitate accurate pose prediction.
elements.sent-2411-280: In a typical tactile image, the contour region is characterized by sharp gradient changes; the non-contact region primarily exhibits scattered, low-gradient noise; and the texture region is determined by the surface and contact conditions of the touched object, typically displaying regular patterns with moderate gradient strength.
elements.sent-2411-281: The algorithm first defines the texture threshold ($T_{texture}$) as the floor value of the average of non-zero gradient values in the gradient map $G$, given by the following equation: \begin{equation}T_{texture} = \left| \frac{1}{|G'|} \sum_{i,j:G_{i,j} \neq 0} G_{i,j} \right| \label{eq:texture}\end{equation} Next, each pixel $(i,j)$ in the image $I$ is smoothed by applying a Gaussian kernel $G_k$, resulting in the smoothed image $S$.
elements.sent-2411-282: Then, the gradient magnitude $G_{i,j}$ and gradient direction $\Theta_{i,j}$ are computed for each pixel.
elements.sent-2411-283: A dual-threshold detection is then performed on these contour points using dynamically adjusted high and low thresholds ($T_{\text{high }$ and $T_{\text{low }$), optimized to match the texture information.}}
elements.sent-2411-284: Through this process, the final set of contour points $C$ is determined.
elements.sent-2411-285: Finally, the Hough Transform is used to extract lines from the contour point set and compute the line direction vectors $H$, providing the necessary data for further processing or analysis.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=15.8cm]{skill_transfer_experiment.pdf} \caption{Skill Transfer Result. Figure (a) illustrates the initial skills in the skill library, while Figure (b) depicts the process of successfully completing the task in new scene after skill transfer. During this process, tactile feedback is integrated into the control loop.} \label{skill_transfer_experiment}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Tactile Contour Extraction Algorithm} \label{alg:AOS} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: A 320x240 Gelsight image $I$ \State Output: Contour point set $C$ and line direction vectors obtained by Hough transform $H$ \Procedure{ContourExtraction}{} \State $T_texture$ $\gets$ \{$G$\} with equation \ref{eq:texture} \For{each pixel $(i, j)$ in $I$} \State $S_{i,j} \gets$ smooth $I$ with $G_k$ \State $G_{i,j} = \sqrt{(S_{i+1,j} - S_{i-1,j})^2 + (S_{i,j+1} - S_{i,j-1})^2}$ \State $\Theta_{i,j} = \arctan\left(\frac{S_{i,j+1} - S_{i,j-1}}{S_{i+1,j} - S_{i-1,j}}\right)$ \EndFor \For{each pixel $(i, j)$ in $G$} \State $N_{i,j} \gets$ \{$G$, $\Theta$\} non-maximum suppression \State $C_{i,j} \gets$ \{$G$, $T_texture$\} dual threshold detection \EndFor \State $T_{\text{high}}, T_{\text{low}} \gets$ adjust with $T_texture$ \State $H \gets$ \{C\} with Hough Transform \EndProcedure \State Return: $C$ and $H$ \end{algorithmic} \label{algorithm}
elements.sent-2411-286: The tactile contour extraction algorithm based on image texture is designed to process a 320x240 resolution Gelsight image and extract contour point sets and line direction vectors using the Hough transform.
elements.sent-2411-287: After obtaining the object's pose relative to the tactile sensor \( q_1 \), its pose in the world coordinate system \( q_w \) is computed as: \begin{equation}q_w = q_3 \otimes q_2 \otimes q_1\end{equation} where \( q_2 \) and \( q_3 \) represent the sensor's installation pose relative to the end-effector and the end-effector's pose in the world frame, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-288: Then the error quaternion \(q_e\) is computed as \begin{equation}q_e = q_t \otimes q_w^{-1}\end{equation} representing the adjustment required for the robotic arm to align the grasped object with the desired pose \(q_t\), thereby providing the command for end-effector correction and improving grasping accuracy and stability.
elements.sent-2411-289: Our experimental platform is built around a KUKA LBR iiwa14 robotic arm.
elements.sent-2411-290: The tactile sensor employed is the Gelsight mini, with sampling frequency of 10Hz.
elements.sent-2411-291: For low-level control, we use position control in the end-effector's Cartesian space at frequency of 100 Hz, enabling the generation of smooth interpolated trajectories.. Implement Details.
elements.sent-2411-292: We use Neo4j to build the knowledge graph-based skill library.
elements.sent-2411-293: We use GPT-4O from the OpenAI API as the Large Language Model.
elements.sent-2411-294: Minimal few-shot prompts are employed to help the LLM understand its role.
elements.sent-2411-295: Additionally, the chain-of-thought technique is used to facilitate deeply understanding of the scene by the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-296: Specific prompts used can be found in the code repository.
elements.sent-2411-297: In our experiment, we set up a drawer and a cabinet with a door, both containing vertically striped cups placed in different positions and orientations.
elements.sent-2411-298: The robot, equipped with a skill library, has already learned to open the drawer, retrieve the cup, move it to a designated location, and complete a stacking task.
elements.sent-2411-299: However, the robot is now presented with a similar but previously unseen scenario, where the skill library does not provide a complete task-specific guidance.
elements.sent-2411-300: Our goal is to enable skill transfer, allowing the robot to autonomously infer the necessary actions in the new environment.
elements.sent-2411-301: Specifically, the robot must open the cabinet door, which involves rotational rather than linear movement, retrieve cups from various positions and orientations, and transport them to the designated location for stacking.
elements.sent-2411-302: In this transfer scenario, the robot is required to complete a sequence of complex sub-tasks, including approaching, grasping, pulling, and releasing the handles of drawers or cabinet doors, followed by approaching, picking up, and moving to a designated position for precise cup stacking.
elements.sent-2411-303: Throughout the process, the robot must adaptively perform trajectory planning tasks based on scene information provided by the skill library.
elements.sent-2411-304: During the cup stacking phase, the robot will adjust its posture adaptively, utilizing tactile perception of physical-level information, such as the contour and orientation of the cup, to successfully complete the stacking task.
elements.sent-2411-305: As shown in Figure~\ref{skill_transfer_experiment , experimental results demonstrate that the robot successfully completed the task in the novel environment without prior training, validating the effectiveness of the proposed hierarchical skill transfer framework in handling long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-306: We compare our method with direct policy transfer method based on reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-307: In complex scenarios, skill transfer and generalization have been key focuses of previous research, primarily achieved through imitation learning from demonstrations and reinforcement learning in simulation \cite{xia2024kinematic .}
elements.sent-2411-308: Imitation learning, however, requires a large amount of data for model pre-training.Therefore, here we choose to compare with a direct policy transfer method based on reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-309: The simulation environment is constructed using Robosuite, an open-source framework for robot simulation and control.
elements.sent-2411-310: Specifically, the direct policy transfer method refers to using the SAC reinforcement learning algorithm \cite{haarnoja2018soft for training in a simulated scenario in advance.}
elements.sent-2411-311: The learned policy network from the SAC algorithm is then directly applied to an untrained door-opening task to guide the robot arm's movements.
elements.sent-2411-312: The design of the reinforcement learning reward function is as follows: \begin{equation}r = \begin{cases} 2 \text{success} 0.25 \times (1 - \tanh(10 \times d)) \text{unsuccess} \end{cases}\end{equation} where success and unsuccess indicate whether the task of opening the drawer is completed, and $d$ represents the Euclidean distance between the robot gripper and the target position on the drawer handle.
elements.sent-2411-313: Training is conducted using the SAC algorithm from stable baselines3 for 1000 episodes, with each episode consisting of a fixed 1000 time steps.
elements.sent-2411-314: The training results are shown in the Figure~\ref{open_drawer , indicating that the reinforcement algorithm has learned the current skill.}
elements.sent-2411-315: The trained policy network from the SAC algorithm is directly transferred and applied to the door-opening task.
elements.sent-2411-316: Ten episodes are tested, each with 1000 steps, while our method is also tested for 10 episodes in the same environment.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=6.5cm]{open_drawer.png} \caption{Results of training the SAC algorithm on the drawer-opening task in simulation environment. } \label{open_drawer}
elements.figure-2411-013: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5} \caption{Skill Transfer Method Success Rate Comparison} \label{tab:success_rate_comparison} \begin{tabular}{lcc} Method Direct policy transfer Ours Suc. Rate 0/10 8/10 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-317: As shown in the TABLE~\ref{tab:success_rate_comparison , compared to the direct policy which struggles to successfully complete tasks in new scenarios, our skill transfer method can maintain a high success rate in new environments.}
elements.sent-2411-318: This is attributed to our skill library and hierarchical skill transfer framework, which can understand tasks and scenarios, and adaptively adjust the execution strategy according to the new scenes during the transfer process.
elements.sent-2411-319: In contrast, policy transfer based on reinforcement learning merely involves transferring actions, making it difficult to adapt to complex and dynamic new scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-320: The goal of this experiment is to evaluate the effectiveness of the object pose perception module in various scenarios, with a focus on the accuracy and reliability of extracting six typical contours.
elements.sent-2411-321: A lower RMSE indicates better algorithm performance.
elements.sent-2411-322: Figure~\ref{fig:experiment_3 shows the results of our algorithm intuitively and Table~\ref{tab:rmse-errors summarizes the RMSE positional errors under different conditions for both algorithms.}}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{experiment_3.pdf} \caption{Tactile adaptive contour extraction results. The blue scatter points represent the extracted contour points, while the green lines represent the straight edges perceived through the Hough line transform applied to the contour points.} \label{fig:experiment_3}
elements.sent-2411-323: Specially, the adaptive algorithm exhibits stronger robustness in scenarios with multiple edges (complex geometries) or contours located at the edge of the sensor’s field of view (challenging contact conditions).
elements.sent-2411-324: Figure~\ref{fig:experiment_3 and the error data clearly demonstrate that our algorithm can effectively perceive the object's pose information, providing valuable insights for further manipulations.}
elements.sent-2411-325: In this work, we propose hierarchical skill transfer framework based on skill library and tactile representation, which efficiently enables skill transfer and generalization across various new scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-326: We construct a skill library based on a knowledge graph, using ``task graphs'' and ``scene graphs'' to represent task and scene knowledge, and ``state graphs'' for interaction.
elements.sent-2411-327: Building on this foundation, we incorporate the skill library to achieve high-level reasoning and subtask transfer at the task level with the help of LLM, adaptive trajectory transfer at the motion level using heuristic path planning algorithms, and adaptive posture adjustment at the physical level based on tactile representation.
elements.sent-2411-328: Real-world experiments have also demonstrated the generalization capability of our framework in practical scenarios.. Limitations.
elements.sent-2411-329: While advantageous, our framework also has limitations.
elements.sent-2411-330: Due to the posture representation and tactile contour extraction algorithm, it cannot model deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-331: Secondly, the currently used LLM is pre-trained on large-scale 2D images and lacks a true understanding of the 3D physical world, which sometimes leads to inaccuracies in motion path generation.
elements.sent-2411-332: This limitation hinders its ability to perform accurate spatial reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-333: Incorporating 3D inputs, such as point clouds, into the fine-tuning process of LLM may alleviate this challenge.. \bibliographystyle{BibTex/IEEEtran \.}
